verses.text: The queen of Sheba travels far to test Solomon with hard questions, and he answers every one. True wisdom draws seekers from the ends of the earth. It is a foretaste of the nations who would come to the light God set in Israel — and to a greater than Solomon.

The queen of Sheba travels far to test Solomon with hard questions, and he answers every one. True wisdom draws seekers from the ends of the earth. It is a foretaste of the nations who would come to the light God set in Israel — and to a greater than Solomon.

The queen of Sheba travels far to test Solomon with hard questions, and he answers every one. True wisdom draws seekers from the ends of the earth. It is a foretaste of the nations who would come to the light God set in Israel — and to a greater than Solomon.

She sees his wisdom, his house, his table, his servants, and his ascent to worship, until 'there was no more spirit in her.' Real glory silences the onlooker. What overwhelms her most is a whole life ordered around the worship of God.

She sees his wisdom, his house, his table, his servants, and his ascent to worship, until 'there was no more spirit in her.' Real glory silences the onlooker. What overwhelms her most is a whole life ordered around the worship of God.

'The half was not told me,' she confesses; the reality far outran the report. And she blesses the LORD who set Solomon to do justice. So those who come to Christ find Him greater than all they heard — and see his throne is God's gift for the good of His people.

Gifts pour both ways — her gold and spices, his bounty in return. Wealth flows in, and Solomon gives generously back. Those whom God enriches are meant to be open-handed; the truest use of plenty is to bless others and to honour God.

Solomon's yearly gold is staggering, and he makes shields of beaten gold for display. The splendour mounts. Yet the law had warned a king not to heap up gold; abundance God gives can quietly become a snare when it grows into mere show.

He makes a great ivory throne overlaid with gold, guarded by lions, 'the like... not made in any kingdom.' The reign reaches its dazzling height. But thrones of ivory and gold pass away; only one throne, the Son of David's, will stand for ever.

Every cup is gold, silver counted as nothing, and all the earth seeks Solomon to hear the wisdom God put in his heart. The source of his greatness is named: God-given wisdom. What truly draws others to us, if anything good, is what God has placed within.

Solomon gathers chariots and horses by the thousand, importing them from Egypt. The wealth is real, but so is the danger: the law forbade the king to multiply horses or return to Egypt. Prosperity, unwatched, leads step by step back toward what God said to leave behind.
